title: Insomnia and Sleep Hygiene
subtitle: The nurse helps a patient with insomnia — teach sleep hygiene first, treat causes, and use sleep medications short-term and cautiously.

patient.name: Insomnia
patient.admitDate: The nurse supports a patient with insomnia.

nursesNote: Insomnia is difficulty falling or staying asleep that causes daytime tiredness and poor function. The nurse first looks for and treats causes such as anxiety, depression, pain, caffeine, or medications. Sleep hygiene is the first-line treatment: a consistent sleep and wake schedule, using the bed only for sleep (and getting up if not sleepy), limiting caffeine, alcohol, and screens in the evening, a relaxing wind-down routine in a dark, quiet room, daytime exercise (not late), and avoiding long naps. Sleep medications are used short-term at the lowest effective dose because of dependence and next-day sedation; the nurse uses fall precautions, especially in older adults.
providerOrders: Assess and treat causes of insomnia
Teach sleep hygiene as the first-line approach
Encourage a consistent sleep and wake schedule
Limit caffeine, alcohol, screens, and long naps
Use sleep medication short-term with fall precautions

opts.text: Insomnia is trouble falling or staying asleep
Causes like anxiety or pain should be treated
Sleep hygiene is the first-line approach
Sleep medications are the first choice long-term
Caffeine in the evening worsens sleep
A consistent schedule helps
Use the bed mainly for sleep
A relaxing wind-down routine helps
Long daytime naps improve night sleep
Exercise earlier in the day helps
Sedatives raise fall risk in older adults
Limit screens before bed

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order helping a patient with insomnia

opts.text: Assess sleep pattern and possible causes
Treat causes like pain or anxiety
Teach sleep hygiene measures
Use short-term medication if needed
Evaluate sleep and document
Going to bed and waking at the same time
Drinking coffee in the evening
A dark, quiet, cool bedroom
Scrolling the phone in bed
A relaxing routine before bed
Taking a long late-afternoon nap

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: An older patient asks for a sleeping pill every night

opts.text: Assess the sleep problem and its cause
Try sleep hygiene measures first
Use medication short-term if needed
Apply fall precautions
Evaluate response and document
'I will keep a regular sleep schedule'
'I will drink coffee at night to relax'
'I will use my bed mainly for sleep'
'I will take sleeping pills every night for years'
'I will dim screens and wind down before bed'
'Long afternoon naps will fix my nights'
'I will exercise earlier in the day'
'A nightcap of alcohol gives the best sleep'
